walkthrough.title: Assign the Forensic Scientist
walkthrough.text: Let's get Deception set up. First, you need to choose one player to be the Forensic Scientist. This person runs the game. They will not have their own cards and they cannot speak during the discussion rounds. Instead, they communicate entirely through scene tiles and bullet markers. Pick someone who is comfortable being a silent game master. If it is your first time, an experienced player makes the best Scientist.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle and Deal Role Cards
walkthrough.text: Now take the role cards. You need one Investigator card for each remaining player, plus exactly one Murderer card. For groups of nine or more, you can also add the Accomplice and Witness cards for extra intrigue, but for your first game, just use Investigators and one Murderer. Shuffle these role cards face down and deal one to each non-Scientist player. Everyone looks at their role secretly. Do not reveal it to anyone.

walkthrough.title: Deal Means and Evidence Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the two decks of clue cards: means cards and evidence cards. Means cards have a red back and describe methods of murder like poison, blunt force, or suffocation. Evidence cards have a blue back and describe items found at the crime scene like a bloody glove, a torn letter, or a cigarette butt. Shuffle each deck separately. Deal four means cards and four evidence cards face up in front of each non-Scientist player. Everyone's cards are visible to the entire table.

walkthrough.title: The Murder Selection
walkthrough.text: Now everyone closes their eyes. The Forensic Scientist instructs the Murderer to open their eyes. The Murderer silently points to one of their own means cards and one of their own evidence cards. These two cards represent the key evidence of the murder — the actual weapon or method used and the critical clue left behind. The Forensic Scientist takes note of both selections. The Murderer closes their eyes, and everyone opens their eyes. The game is afoot.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Scene Tiles
walkthrough.text: The Forensic Scientist now sets up their scene tiles. There are two mandatory tiles that are always used: Cause of Death and Location of Crime. These go face up on the table. Then shuffle the remaining optional scene tiles and draw four at random, placing them face down near the Scientist. These will be revealed one per round to give the investigators additional clues.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Badge Tokens
walkthrough.text: Give each non-Scientist player one badge token. This badge represents your one formal guess during the entire game. Once you spend it, you cannot make another formal accusation. Place your badge face up in front of you so everyone can see who still has theirs. The Forensic Scientist does not get a badge.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Bullet Markers
walkthrough.text: Give the Forensic Scientist all of the bullet markers. These are small round tokens that the Scientist places on the scene tiles to communicate clues. The Scientist places one bullet marker on each option on the Cause of Death and Location tiles to indicate the correct answers. This is the Scientist's only way to communicate — no talking, no gestures, no facial expressions. Just bullet markers on tiles.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Play
walkthrough.text: Setup is complete. The Cause of Death and Location tiles should have their bullet markers placed. Each non-Scientist player has four means cards and four evidence cards face up, plus one badge token. The Forensic Scientist has their remaining scene tiles ready. The Murderer knows which two cards they selected, and everyone else is trying to figure it out. Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Deception is a social deduction game played over approximately three rounds. A murder has been committed, and the Forensic Scientist knows exactly who did it and how. The investigators are trying to identify the Murderer and the two key clue cards — one means and one evidence — from among all the cards on the table. The Murderer is hiding among the investigators, participating in discussion and trying to throw everyone off the scent. The Scientist can only communicate by placing bullet markers on scene tiles.

walkthrough.title: The Forensic Scientist's Role
walkthrough.text: The Forensic Scientist is the heart of the game but has severe restrictions. They cannot speak at any time during the game. They cannot nod, shake their head, point at players, or make any gestures that could be interpreted as hints. Their only communication tool is placing bullet markers on scene tiles. Each scene tile has a category like Cause of Death, Location, or Weather, and each category has several options. The Scientist places a bullet marker on the option that best fits the murder. The challenge is that the options rarely describe the murder perfectly, so the Scientist must choose the closest fit.

walkthrough.title: Round Structure
walkthrough.text: The game flows through roughly three rounds. In the first round, the Scientist has already placed bullets on the Cause of Death and Location tiles. Players discuss openly, examining everyone's face-up cards and debating theories. There is no turn order for discussion — everyone talks freely. At the end of the discussion period, the Scientist reveals one new scene tile from the face-down stack and places a bullet marker on it. In the second round, players discuss again with this new information. Then the Scientist reveals another tile. The third round works the same way. After three rounds of discussion, the game enters the final phase.

walkthrough.title: Discussion Rules
walkthrough.text: During discussion, all non-Scientist players talk freely. There is no turn order and no time limit by default, though groups often set a timer for each round to keep things moving. You can point at other players' cards, suggest theories, ask questions, and build on others' arguments. The Murderer participates fully in these discussions. In fact, the Murderer should be actively discussing and suggesting theories to deflect suspicion. The only person who cannot participate in the conversation is the Forensic Scientist.

walkthrough.title: Making a Formal Guess
walkthrough.text: At any point during the game, a player may spend their badge token to make a formal guess. To do so, you must name a specific player as the Murderer AND identify exactly which means card and which evidence card from that player's display are the murder weapons. All three must be correct for the guess to succeed. If correct, the investigators win immediately. If wrong, you lose your badge and cannot guess again for the rest of the game. You still participate in discussion, but you have no more guesses. This is why timing your guess is critical — guess too early with insufficient information and you waste your one shot.

walkthrough.title: Winning as Investigators
walkthrough.text: The investigators win if any player makes a correct formal guess at any point during the game. Remember, a correct guess requires naming the right player, the right means card, and the right evidence card. All three. If any one of those is wrong, the guess fails. This means even if you correctly identify the Murderer but pick the wrong evidence card, your guess still fails and your badge is spent.

walkthrough.title: Winning as the Murderer
walkthrough.text: The Murderer wins if the game ends without any investigator making a correct formal guess. This happens when all rounds of discussion are complete and either no one used their badge, or all guesses were incorrect. The Murderer's strategy is to blend in with the investigators, suggest plausible theories that point away from their own cards, and subtly support incorrect guesses by other players. A good Murderer looks like they are genuinely trying to solve the case.

walkthrough.title: The Forensic Scientist's Tile Strategy
walkthrough.text: The Forensic Scientist needs to think carefully about which option on each tile to mark. The tiles often have imperfect options. For example, the Cause of Death tile might list Severe Injury, Loss of Blood, Poisoning, Illness, Suffocation, and Accident. If the Murderer chose poison as their means card, the Scientist would likely mark Poisoning. But sometimes the connection is more abstract. The Scientist is trying to guide investigators toward both the correct means and evidence cards, so each bullet placement should ideally narrow the field.

walkthrough.title: Replacing a Scene Tile
walkthrough.text: Once during the game, the Forensic Scientist may choose to replace one of their previously placed scene tiles with a new one from the stack. This is useful when a tile's options are so poor that the bullet marker is misleading investigators rather than helping. The Scientist removes the old tile and its marker, draws a new tile, and places a fresh bullet marker. This action replaces the normal new tile reveal for that round, so use it wisely.

walkthrough.title: No Player Elimination
walkthrough.text: One of the best features of Deception is that no one is ever eliminated. Even if you make a wrong formal guess and lose your badge, you stay in the game. You can still discuss, debate, and influence others. You just cannot make another formal accusation. This keeps everyone engaged from start to finish. The Murderer is never revealed until the game ends, so even a player who has been accused stays in the discussion.

walkthrough.title: Optional Role: Accomplice
walkthrough.text: The Accomplice is on the Murderer's team. During the eyes-closed phase, the Accomplice also opens their eyes and sees who the Murderer is and which cards were selected. The Accomplice then participates in discussion like a normal investigator, but secretly works to misdirect. The Accomplice wins if the Murderer wins. The Accomplice does not know who the Murderer selected cards from until they see the pointing — they learn everything the Scientist does.

walkthrough.title: Optional Role: Witness
walkthrough.text: The Witness is also added for larger groups and is on the investigators' team. During the eyes-closed phase, after the Murderer closes their eyes, the Witness opens their eyes and the Forensic Scientist points to the Murderer. So the Witness knows who the Murderer is, but does not know which specific cards were selected. Here is the catch: if the investigators fail to solve the case and the Murderer wins, the Murderer gets one chance to identify the Witness. If the Murderer correctly names the Witness, the evil team wins completely. If wrong, the Witness survives. This creates tension for the Witness — they want to help, but revealing too much knowledge makes them a target.

walkthrough.title: Game End Summary
walkthrough.text: The game ends in one of three ways. First, an investigator makes a correct formal guess — investigators win. Second, all discussion rounds end with no correct guess — the Murderer wins. Third, if the Witness role is in play and the Murderer wins, the Murderer attempts to identify the Witness. If successful, the evil team wins decisively. If the Murderer guesses wrong about the Witness, the game is a partial victory for the evil side but the Witness survives. After the game ends, reveal all roles and discuss the clues. This debrief is often the most entertaining part of the game.

Investigators try to identify the Murderer + correct means + correct evidence
Murderer hides among investigators, misdirecting discussion

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Crime Scene
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted game with six players. One player is the Forensic Scientist, one is the Murderer, and the other four are Investigators. We will follow the action step by step so you can see how the discussion flows, how the Scientist communicates through tiles, and how investigators piece together clues. Even if your group is a different size, the mechanics work the same way.

walkthrough.title: Roles Are Dealt
walkthrough.text: Everyone has looked at their secret role card. The Forensic Scientist asks all players to close their eyes. The Murderer opens their eyes and looks at their eight face-up cards — four means and four evidence. The Murderer points to Rope as their means card and Broken Glass as their evidence card. The Scientist nods silently, takes note, and the Murderer closes their eyes. Everyone opens their eyes. The game begins.

walkthrough.title: Scene Tiles Are Set
walkthrough.text: The Forensic Scientist places bullet markers on the two mandatory tiles. On the Cause of Death tile, the options include Severe Injury, Loss of Blood, Poisoning, Illness, Suffocation, and Accident. The Scientist places a bullet on Suffocation — rope is a strangling weapon, and Suffocation is the closest fit. On the Location tile, options include Bedroom, Hospital, Playground, Classroom, Bar, and Abandoned Building. The Scientist places a bullet on Abandoned Building, suggesting a desolate setting where broken glass might be found.

walkthrough.title: Round 1 Discussion Begins
walkthrough.text: The investigators start discussing. Investigator A says: Suffocation plus Abandoned Building — that sounds like someone was strangled in a run-down place. I am looking for anything related to strangling or choking across everyone's cards. Investigator B scans the table and spots that the Murderer has Rope in their display. Investigator B says: Hey, Player 3 has Rope as a means card. That fits Suffocation perfectly. The Murderer stays calm.

walkthrough.title: The Murderer Deflects
walkthrough.text: The Murderer needs to respond without looking suspicious. They say: Sure, Rope could fit, but Player 5 has Plastic Bag as a means card — that fits Suffocation too. And look at Player 2's Wire — you can strangle someone with wire. The Murderer is doing their job — acknowledging the obvious connection but spreading suspicion across multiple players. This is classic Murderer strategy: do not deny, redirect.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Rushing the Formal Guess
walkthrough.text: Investigator A gets excited. They say: I am pretty sure it is Player 3 with the Rope. I am going to use my badge now. Wait! This is a common beginner mistake. We are still in Round 1 with only two scene tiles revealed. Investigator A has identified a likely means card but has not narrowed down the evidence card at all. Player 3 has four evidence cards — Broken Glass, Torn Fabric, Fingerprints, and Muddy Shoes. Guessing the wrong evidence card wastes the badge even if the Murderer and means are correct. Hold your badge until you have more information.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Save Your Badge
walkthrough.text: The key lesson here is that your formal guess must identify all three elements correctly — the player, their means card, and their evidence card. Two out of three is still a failed guess and a wasted badge. In a six-player game, each player has four evidence cards. Even if you are certain about the Murderer and their means card, you still have a one-in-four chance of picking the right evidence. Wait for more scene tiles to narrow it down before spending your badge.

walkthrough.title: Round 2: New Tile Revealed
walkthrough.text: The Forensic Scientist reveals a new scene tile: Weather. The options are Sunny, Stormy, Windy, Dry, Cold, and Foggy. The Scientist places a bullet on Stormy. Why? A storm at night in an abandoned building — maybe the broken glass was a window shattered by the storm, or rain drove the victim inside. The investigators now have another piece to work with.

walkthrough.title: Connecting the Clues
walkthrough.text: Investigator C starts connecting dots. They say: Suffocation, Abandoned Building, Stormy weather. The victim was strangled in a derelict building during a storm. What evidence fits a stormy abandoned building? I am looking for things related to weather damage or debris. Investigator B adds: Player 3 has Broken Glass as an evidence card. A storm could shatter windows in an abandoned building. That is two cards from Player 3 that fit perfectly — Rope and Broken Glass.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Forgetting the Evidence Card
walkthrough.text: Investigator D has been focused entirely on the means cards. They say: I think it is Player 5 with the Plastic Bag — suffocation fits perfectly! But when asked which evidence card, Investigator D has no idea. They have been so fixated on matching means cards to Suffocation that they completely ignored the evidence half of the puzzle. Remember, you need both cards. The scene tiles are clues pointing toward both the means and the evidence. Always ask yourself: which evidence card does this tile support?

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Track Both Cards
walkthrough.text: Every scene tile the Forensic Scientist reveals is a clue about the murder as a whole — not just the weapon. When you see a new bullet marker, ask yourself two questions: which means cards does this support, and which evidence cards does this support? The strongest theories connect a specific player's means card AND evidence card to the scene tile pattern. If your theory only explains one card, it is incomplete.

walkthrough.title: Round 3: Another Tile
walkthrough.text: The Scientist reveals another tile: Trace Evidence. Options include Fingerprint, Blood, Hair, Fiber, Footprint, and Shard. The Scientist places a bullet on Shard — directly pointing at Broken Glass. Now the pattern is strong: Suffocation, Abandoned Building, Stormy, Shard. The investigators have four data points all converging on one player's cards.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Scientist Breaks Silence
walkthrough.text: At this point, let's talk about a common Forensic Scientist mistake. Imagine the Scientist accidentally nods when Investigator B mentions Broken Glass. Or they wince when someone suggests the wrong player. Or they lean forward excitedly when the right theory is discussed. Any of these reactions break the rules. The Forensic Scientist must maintain a completely neutral expression and body language at all times. Even involuntary reactions can give unfair information. If you are the Scientist, practice your poker face before the game starts.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: The Scientist Is Silent
walkthrough.text: The Forensic Scientist's only communication channel is bullet markers on tiles. No words, no sounds, no gestures, no facial expressions. This is what makes the role challenging and fun. If you catch the Scientist reacting, gently remind them. And if you are the Scientist, keep a neutral face no matter what happens in the discussion. Some groups even have the Scientist look down at their tiles to avoid accidental eye contact that could be interpreted as a hint.

walkthrough.title: Building the Case
walkthrough.text: The investigators now have a strong theory. Investigator B lays it out: Suffocation points to Rope. Abandoned Building and Stormy suggest a desolate location with weather damage. Shard points directly to Broken Glass. Player 3 has both Rope and Broken Glass. Everything lines up. Investigator C agrees. Even the Murderer has to agree it looks compelling — denying too hard would look suspicious.

walkthrough.title: The Formal Guess
walkthrough.text: Investigator B is ready. They turn in their badge token and make their formal guess: I accuse Player 3. The means is Rope. The evidence is Broken Glass. The Forensic Scientist confirms — all three are correct! The investigators win! Notice that Investigator B waited until Round 3 when they had four scene tiles worth of information before spending their badge. This patience is what separates successful investigators from wasted badges.

walkthrough.title: The Debrief
walkthrough.text: After the game, everyone reveals their roles and talks about the clues. The Scientist explains why they chose each bullet marker. The Murderer explains their deflection strategy. This debrief is one of the best parts of Deception — you get to see how each person interpreted the clues and where the Murderer almost got away with it. Take a few minutes for this conversation before shuffling up for another round.

walkthrough.title: Before You Investigate
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your own game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategies on reading the Scientist's clues and deflecting as the Murderer.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to roles, card types, and tile mechanics.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Investigate

Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for roles and tiles
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Reading the Scene Tiles
walkthrough.text: The Forensic Scientist is trying to communicate a story through imperfect options. Do not take each tile in isolation — instead, look for patterns across all the tiles together. Cause of Death, Location, Weather, and other tiles paint a picture of the crime scene as a whole. When a new tile is revealed, do not just ask what it means on its own. Ask how it connects to the tiles already on the table. The strongest deductions come from linking multiple tiles to a specific pair of cards.

walkthrough.title: Scanning the Table Efficiently
walkthrough.text: With four or more players, there can be over thirty cards on the table. Scanning all of them is overwhelming. Start by looking at the Cause of Death tile — it usually narrows the means cards significantly. If it says Poisoning, mentally highlight every poison-related means card on the table. Then use the Location and other tiles to filter down the evidence cards. Work from the most restrictive clue to the least restrictive. This funnel approach is much more effective than trying to evaluate every card combination.

walkthrough.title: When to Use Your Badge
walkthrough.text: Your badge is your most precious resource. Never spend it on a guess you are not at least fairly confident about on all three elements — player, means, and evidence. A good rule of thumb: wait until at least three scene tiles have been revealed before making your formal guess. If multiple investigators are converging on the same theory, let the most confident one use their badge. There is no bonus for guessing first. A patient, well-informed guess wins games.

walkthrough.title: Playing as the Murderer
walkthrough.text: The Murderer's biggest mistake is going silent. If you stop talking or get defensive, experienced investigators will notice. Instead, be an active participant. Suggest theories confidently — just make sure they point at the wrong player. The best tactic is to build a plausible case against someone else. Find another player whose cards have some connection to the scene tiles and champion that theory. Agree with investigators who are on the wrong track and subtly cast doubt on those who are getting close to you. Never directly say someone is wrong — instead, propose a more compelling alternative.

walkthrough.title: Playing as the Forensic Scientist
walkthrough.text: Your job is the hardest in the game. You know exactly who the Murderer is and which cards they chose, but you can only communicate through bullet markers on tiles with limited options. Prioritize the Cause of Death tile — it gives the strongest signal about the means card. For evidence cards, you often need the optional tiles to provide context. When placing bullets, think about what the investigators will naturally connect. Avoid options that could equally point to multiple players' cards. The more specific your bullet placements, the fewer false leads you create.

walkthrough.title: Common Traps to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Here are the traps that catch new players. First, tunnel vision: locking onto one suspect too early and ignoring evidence that does not fit your theory. Always consider at least two suspects until the tiles give you a clear signal. Second, forgetting evidence cards: means cards get most of the attention because Cause of Death points at them directly, but evidence cards are equally important for a correct guess. Third, social pressure: agreeing with the loudest person at the table instead of forming your own theory. The Murderer is often the loudest voice because they are trying to control the narrative.

walkthrough.title: Communication as an Investigator
walkthrough.text: Good investigators share their reasoning, not just their conclusions. Instead of saying I think it is Player 3, say I think it is Player 3 because Suffocation maps to Rope and the Shard trace evidence maps to Broken Glass. When you explain your logic, other investigators can build on it, challenge weak links, and refine the theory. This collaborative reasoning is how investigators overcome the Murderer's misdirection. A team that shares logic catches the killer. A team that shouts accusations does not.

walkthrough.title: Adapting to Player Count
walkthrough.text: The game plays differently at different player counts. With four or five players, there are fewer cards on the table, so the Scientist's clues narrow things down faster. Games tend to be shorter and more focused. With eight or more players, the table is flooded with cards and the Murderer can hide more easily. In large groups, pay extra attention to the Scientist's tile choices because there are more false matches to sort through. Also consider using the Accomplice and Witness roles in large groups to add another layer of deduction and tension.

summary.title: Reading the Scene Tiles
bullets: Look for patterns across ALL tiles, not each tile in isolation
Ask: how does this new tile connect to the ones already revealed?
Strongest deductions link multiple tiles to one pair of cards

summary.title: Scanning the Table Efficiently
bullets: Start with Cause of Death — narrows means cards the most
Then use Location and other tiles to filter evidence cards
Work from most restrictive clue to least restrictive

summary.title: When to Use Your Badge
bullets: Wait until at least 3 scene tiles are revealed
Must be confident in all 3 elements: player + means + evidence
No bonus for guessing first — patience wins games

summary.title: Playing as the Murderer
bullets: Stay active in discussion — silence is suspicious
Build a plausible case against another player
Never deny directly — propose a more compelling alternative
Support investigators who are on the wrong track

summary.title: Playing as the Forensic Scientist
bullets: Cause of Death is your strongest signal for the means card
Use optional tiles to provide context for the evidence card
Choose options that point to as few false matches as possible

summary.title: Common Traps to Avoid
bullets: Tunnel vision: consider at least 2 suspects until tiles are clear
Forgetting evidence: means gets attention but evidence is equally critical
Social pressure: form your own theory, don't follow the loudest voice

summary.title: Communication as an Investigator
bullets: Share reasoning, not just conclusions — explain your logic
Other investigators can build on, challenge, or refine your theory
Collaborative reasoning beats shouting accusations

summary.title: Adapting to Player Count
bullets: 4-5 players: fewer cards, faster deduction, shorter games
8+ players: more cards, Murderer hides easier, more false matches
Large groups: consider Accomplice and Witness for added tension

walkthrough.title: Advanced Murderer: The Helpful Detective
walkthrough.text: The most dangerous Murderers are the ones who seem like the most helpful investigators. Instead of staying quiet or getting defensive, actively lead the discussion. Pick another player whose cards have a superficial connection to the scene tiles and build a convincing case against them. Use phrases like let me walk through the logic here and if you look at the tiles together. The goal is to make the table think you are the best detective in the room. When someone starts suspecting you, do not panic — calmly acknowledge the connection and then offer a stronger alternative theory. The table follows confidence.

walkthrough.title: Advanced Murderer: Card Selection Strategy
walkthrough.text: Choose a means card that has multiple plausible matches across other players' displays. If three different players have strangling-type means cards, choosing Rope is safer than choosing something unique to you. Similarly, pick an evidence card that is generic enough that the Scientist's tile clues could plausibly point at other players' evidence cards too. The harder it is for the Scientist to uniquely identify your cards through tiles, the safer you are.

walkthrough.title: Advanced Scientist: Tile Prioritization
walkthrough.text: Not all scene tiles are created equal. When you draw your optional tiles, mentally rank them by how well they can distinguish the murder cards from the other cards on the table. A tile about Weather might not help at all if it does not connect to either the means or evidence card. But a tile about Trace Evidence with a Shard option when the evidence card is Broken Glass is incredibly powerful. If your drawn tiles are poor, remember you can swap one tile during the game. Save that swap for when a tile is actively misleading the investigators rather than just being unhelpful.

walkthrough.title: Advanced Scientist: Dual-Card Signaling
walkthrough.text: The best Forensic Scientists place each bullet marker with both cards in mind. When you mark an option on a tile, ask yourself: does this help narrow down the means card, the evidence card, or ideally both? For example, if the murder was committed with Rope and the evidence is Broken Glass, marking Suffocation on Cause of Death clearly signals Rope. But marking Shard on Trace Evidence signals Broken Glass. The ideal tile choices create a narrative that only makes sense if investigators look at the correct pair of cards together.

walkthrough.title: Reading Behavioral Tells
walkthrough.text: While the formal rules only involve scene tiles and discussion, experienced players watch behavior closely. The Murderer often shows subtle tells. Watch for players who aggressively push theories about others — especially if their enthusiasm seems disproportionate to the evidence. Notice who changes the subject when discussion gets too close to their cards. Pay attention to players who agree with wrong theories a little too quickly. These behavioral reads are not proof, but they can help you decide between two equally plausible suspects.

walkthrough.title: The Elimination Method
walkthrough.text: Instead of trying to identify the Murderer, work backward by eliminating suspects. Look at each player's cards and ask: can the scene tiles possibly describe this player's cards? If a player has no means card that fits the Cause of Death tile and no evidence card that fits the other tiles, they are probably innocent. Cross them off your mental list. With four or five players, eliminating even one or two suspects dramatically improves your odds. This method is especially powerful in the later rounds when you have four or more tile data points.

walkthrough.title: Witness and Accomplice Mind Games
walkthrough.text: When playing with the Witness and Accomplice, the social dynamics shift dramatically. The Witness knows who the Murderer is but must be careful not to reveal that knowledge too openly, or the Murderer will identify them after the game and steal the win. The Witness should subtly guide discussion toward the correct suspect without being the first to name them. The Accomplice, on the other hand, should appear to be a helpful investigator while steering the group away from the Murderer. In large groups, watch for pairs of players who seem to be coordinating — one of them might be the Accomplice.

walkthrough.title: Meta-Strategy: Controlling the Narrative
walkthrough.text: The most advanced skill in Deception is narrative control. Whoever frames the discussion wins. As an investigator, set the agenda by establishing a clear framework: Let us go tile by tile and see which player's cards match. This systematic approach is harder for the Murderer to derail. As the Murderer, control the narrative by being the first to propose a theory each round. When you set the framework, you get to choose which comparisons the table makes. The player who speaks first and speaks with structure shapes how everyone else thinks about the clues.

summary.title: Advanced Murderer: The Helpful Detective
bullets: Act like the best detective at the table — lead discussion actively
Build convincing cases against other players with tile connections
When suspected, calmly acknowledge and offer a stronger alternative
The table follows confidence, not silence

summary.title: Advanced Murderer: Card Selection Strategy
bullets: Choose means/evidence cards that overlap with other players' displays
Generic cards = harder for Scientist to uniquely signal them
Unique cards are easier to identify — avoid them as Murderer

summary.title: Advanced Scientist: Tile Prioritization
bullets: Rank optional tiles by how well they distinguish murder cards
Save the tile-swap ability for when a tile is actively misleading
A tile that doesn't connect to either card is nearly useless

summary.title: Advanced Scientist: Dual-Card Signaling
bullets: Each bullet should help narrow the means, evidence, or both
Create a narrative that only makes sense with the correct card pair
Best tiles point toward both cards simultaneously

summary.title: Reading Behavioral Tells
bullets: Watch for disproportionate enthusiasm about wrong theories
Notice who changes subject when discussion nears their cards
Players who agree with wrong theories too quickly may be the Murderer
Behavioral reads help decide between equally plausible suspects

summary.title: The Elimination Method
bullets: Work backward: eliminate players whose cards don't match any tiles
Eliminating 1-2 suspects dramatically improves odds
Most powerful in later rounds with 4+ tile data points

summary.title: Witness and Accomplice Mind Games
bullets: Witness: guide toward correct suspect subtly, don't be first to name them
Accomplice: appear helpful while steering away from the Murderer
Watch for coordinating pairs — one might be the Accomplice

summary.title: Meta-Strategy: Controlling the Narrative
bullets: Investigators: use systematic tile-by-tile framework to prevent Murderer derailing
Murderers: speak first each round to set the discussion framework
Whoever frames the discussion shapes how everyone interprets clues

entries.definition: The game master role. Knows the Murderer's identity and selected cards. Cannot speak, gesture, or react during the game. Communicates only by placing bullet markers on scene tile options. Does not have their own means or evidence cards.

The hidden killer. During the eyes-closed phase, secretly selects one means card and one evidence card from their own display. Participates in discussion as if they were an investigator, trying to misdirect the group. Wins if no correct formal guess is made by game end.

The detective role assigned to most players. Investigators discuss the scene tiles and each other's cards to identify the Murderer and the two selected cards. Each investigator has one badge token for a single formal guess.

Optional role for 9+ players. On the Murderer's team. Sees the Murderer and selected cards during the eyes-closed phase. Pretends to be an investigator while secretly misdirecting the group. Wins if the Murderer wins.

Optional role for 9+ players. On the investigators' team. Knows who the Murderer is but not which cards were selected. If the Murderer wins, the Murderer gets one chance to identify the Witness — if correct, evil wins completely.

Cards with red backs describing methods or weapons of murder, such as Rope, Poison, Knife, Blunt Force, or Suffocation. Each non-Scientist player has four means cards face up in their display. The Murderer selects one as the murder weapon.

Cards with blue backs describing items or clues found at the crime scene, such as Broken Glass, Torn Fabric, Fingerprints, or Cigarette Butt. Each non-Scientist player has four evidence cards face up. The Murderer selects one as the key evidence.

Large tiles with a category name and multiple options. The Forensic Scientist places one bullet marker on each tile to indicate which option best describes the murder. Scene tiles are the Scientist's only communication tool.

Small round tokens placed by the Forensic Scientist on scene tile options. One bullet per tile. The placement communicates the Scientist's clue about the murder. Bullets cannot be moved once placed, unless the Scientist uses their one-time tile swap.

A player spends their badge token and names a specific player, one of that player's means cards, and one of their evidence cards. All three must be correct for the investigators to win. A wrong guess wastes the badge permanently but the player continues discussing.

Each non-Scientist player receives one badge token during setup. The badge is spent to make a formal guess. Once spent, it cannot be recovered. A player without a badge can still participate in discussion but cannot make another formal accusation.

One of the two mandatory scene tiles, always used in every game. Options typically include Severe Injury, Loss of Blood, Poisoning, Illness, Suffocation, and Accident. The Scientist marks the option that best matches the selected means card.

One of the two mandatory scene tiles, always used in every game. Options typically include various settings like Bedroom, Hospital, Playground, Bar, and Abandoned Building. The Scientist marks the option that best fits the murder scenario.

Additional scene tiles drawn randomly each game, covering categories like Weather, Trace Evidence, Victim's Build, Time of Crime, Motive, and more. Four are drawn face-down and revealed one per round to provide additional clues.

When the Witness role is in play, if the investigators fail to make a correct guess, the Murderer attempts to identify the Witness. The Murderer points to one player. If correct, the Murderer's team wins completely. If wrong, the Witness survives. This creates tension for the Witness — help too obviously and you become a target.

The phase at the start of the game where all players close their eyes. The Forensic Scientist instructs the Murderer (and optionally Accomplice) to open their eyes and make selections. The Witness also opens their eyes separately to learn the Murderer's identity. This phase establishes all hidden information.

Once per game, the Forensic Scientist may remove a previously placed scene tile and replace it with a new one from the unused stack. A new bullet marker is placed on the replacement tile. This replaces the normal tile reveal for that round. Used when a tile is misleading investigators.

A period of open discussion where all non-Scientist players debate theories about the murder. There is no turn order — everyone talks freely. At the end of each round, the Forensic Scientist reveals a new scene tile. Players may make formal guesses at any point during discussion.
